description: Head-to-head: line-Jaccard vs cursor-agent (composer-2.5) read-only judge on a stratified sample of pairs. Ground truth: test-outcome Jaccard ≥ 0.999 (verifier sees both trials identically).

comparison.threshold_note: Per-metric threshold maximizes balanced accuracy on this sample (TPR + TNR) / 2; precision/recall reported at that cutoff.

pairs.agent_reasoning: Both patches fix the same bug by passing multi_class into the temporary LogisticRegression inside _log_reg_scoring_path, so multinomial LogisticRegressionCV scoring uses softmax probabilities instead of OvR. The only library code difference is that A also forwards random_state to that helper instance, which is never used because fit is not called before scoring. Extra ad-hoc test scripts in A do not change sklearn runtime behavior.

Both patches add the same guard in ConditionSet._eval_subs when substitution yields cond is S.true. Patch A routes through ConditionSet(sym, S.true, base), which __new__ immediately reduces to base_set; patch B returns base directly. That is the only library change and matches the issue-19495 fix, so observable substitution behavior is the same on all inputs where the condition is exactly S.true.

Both skip Value() wrapping for F(), combined F expressions, and Func-based expressions, fixing the same bulk_update bug. The sole logic difference is expression detection: isinstance(Expression, F) versus hasattr(resolve_expression), which only diverges for rare BaseExpression types like Subquery. Test changes are cosmetic variants of the same F() scenarios.

Both patches make the same change to k_means(): pre-generate per-init seeds once, use them in the n_jobs==1 loop, and remove the duplicate seeds line in the parallel branch. The git diffs against base are identical and the A/ and B/ trees match, so they behave the same on all inputs; only the edit sequence differs.

Both patches replace the hardcoded 70/1900–2000 century split in parse_http_date with the same RFC 7231 sliding 50-year window logic, differing only in whether the anchor year is taken from utcnow() (A) or now() (B). That one-line choice is behaviorally equivalent in many common cases but diverges for two-digit years when local and UTC years differ or when tests pin utcnow to 2019/2020/2048 while B still reads the real local year. A matches the gold fix and intended GMT-relative behavior; B is the same fix idea with a wrong clock reference.

Both patches apply the identical change to sklearn/cluster/k_means_.py: pre-generate n_init integer seeds once, iterate for seed in seeds in the serial path with random_state=seed, and reuse the same seeds in the parallel branch. A/ and B/ trees are byte-identical in the patched file, fixing the bug where n_jobs=1 reused one RNG state per init while n_jobs>1 used distinct seeds.

Both patches fix the same bug by changing ConciseDateFormatter's show_offset suppression from level<2 (years and months) to year-level only. Patch A uses `level == 0` and patch B uses `level < 1`, which are equivalent for the integer level values 0–5 produced by the loop. The accompanying test updates are identical between the two trees.

Both patches replace the same hardcoded year<70 branch in parse_http_date with identical RFC 7231 sliding-window logic using now().year and the same century adjustment. The sole textual difference is year > current_year + 50 versus year - current_year > 50, which are algebraically the same for integer years. No other files differ, so every HTTP date input yields the same parsed timestamp.

Patches A and B apply the same one-line logic change (suppress offset only for year-level ticks, not months) and the same test expectation update. The applied trees are identical in the two modified files, so runtime behavior matches on all inputs.

Both patches only change p_product_of_units in cds.py to correctly distinguish PRODUCT ('.') from DIVISION ('/') when len(p)==4, with identical multiply, divide, and unary-inverse semantics. PLY stores lexeme strings as token values, so A's p[2]=='.' branch and B's p[2]=='/' branch are logically complementary and equivalent for all grammar cases. No other files differ between A and B.

Both patches fix the same bug in Add._eval_is_zero by replacing the boolean im flag with a list of imaginary terms and, when real parts cancel, checking whether the imaginary parts cancel instead of always returning False. The sole semantic difference is whether cancellation is tested on a/I (A) or on the raw imaginary terms (B); for is_imaginary args these are equivalent in principle but may differ slightly on unevaluated symbolic sums due to different simplification paths.

The production fix in astropy/io/fits/diff.py is byte-identical in both trees: Q-format VLA columns join the same per-element comparison path as P-format columns. Patch B only adds a regression test in test_diff.py, which does not change runtime FITS-diff behavior. For all user-facing inputs, including Q-format VLA tables, the two patches produce the same observable results.

Both patches change the same offset-hiding logic in ConciseDateFormatter.format_ticks: hide the offset at year level (level 0), and at month level (level 1) only when a January tick (month==1) will show the year in a label. Patch A uses nested if level < 2 / level == 0 / else; patch B uses if level == 0 / elif level == 1 with the identical January check. No other files differ; observable tick/offset formatting is the same for all inputs.

Both fix the core bug: skipped unittest tests with --pdb no longer trigger pytest's deferred tearDown when @unittest.skip is used. A clears _explicit_tearDown in addSkip; B never sets it for __unittest_skip__ tests in runtest. Those differ on runtime SkipTest with --pdb, and A alone patches assertion rewrite lineno on Python 3.11+.

Both replace the broken `"P" in col.format` check with row-wise VLA comparison, fixing Q-format (QD) false positives—the core issue. They diverge on detection (object dtype vs `_format.p_format`) and on non-float VLAs, where B uses `array_equal` while A always uses `allclose`. Patch A also changes unrelated `quantity_helper` numpy dispatch, so full-program behavior is not identical even though the FITS diff fix is largely aligned.

Both fix the same total-degree filtering bug (max exponent vs sum of exponents) in itermonomials, but B is a two-line change on the old algorithm while A rewrites generation by exact degree and fixes empty-variable handling. For typical calls with real variables they should agree, yet they diverge when variables is empty and min_degree>0 (B yields S.One, A yields nothing), and they rely on different enumeration logic that could differ on rarer edge inputs.

Both target the same urlize trim_punctuation bug with algorithms that match on the bug scenarios and fuzzed inputs, but patch A introduces a SyntaxError by merging trim_url and trim_punctuation onto one line, so the program cannot run at all whereas B applies a working fix. Observable behavior therefore diverges on every input, not just edge cases.

Both patches fix the same deferred-loading bug by ensuring relation traversal uses the concrete model's _meta rather than a proxy's. On typical inputs (concrete or MTI related models) they produce identical cur_model and opts. They differ only in where .concrete_model is applied: A adjusts opts globally while B adjusts cur_model on forward FKs only, which can diverge for rare proxy-model relation paths and reverse-O2O branches.

Both candidates apply the identical one-line production fix in TableDataDiff, extending the VLA branch to include Q-format columns alongside P-format. diff.py is byte-identical between A and B. B adds a regression test only; that does not change FITSDiff behavior on any user input.

Both patches apply the same single-line production fix: adding Combinable.MOD to the mixed-type numeric section of _connector_combinations in expressions.py, and that file is identical between A and B. The only diff is that A also extends a unit test to exercise MOD alongside ADD/SUB/MUL/DIV; B omits that test change. Test-only differences do not change runtime behavior for modulo expressions with mixed numeric operands.

Both remove the multi-axis min_count ValueError and use the same counting logic for partial reductions with ndim > 0, but only A changes duck_array_ops (routing min_count to nan reducers). Their nanops control flows differ: A regresses single-axis scalar nulling and treats 0-d multi-axis results differently from B, which keeps base duck_array_ops and preserves scalar full-reduction behavior.

Both fix urlize() trim_punctuation when HTML entities precede trailing punctuation, and they agree on the standard </<! cases that motivated the bug. Patch A removes html.escape(suffix) in one step with a fallback to the original buggy slice logic; patch B pops one escaped character per unescaped punctuation char removed. They can diverge on edge cases where the escaped suffix does not match the end of middle (A falls back) or where popping alters unescape length non-uniformly (B's fixed iteration count).

Both patches parenthesize multi-element tuple literals and strip parentheses when unparsing tuple subscript slices, matching on the main regression cases. They diverge on one-element tuples—A yields (x) while B correctly yields (x,)—and on Python <3.9 ASTs where A unwraps ast.Index in subscripts but B can add spurious parentheses around Tuple[...] slices.

Both patches apply the identical one-line fix in generate_renamed_fields, using model_name instead of old_model_name when looking up the new model state after a model rename. The only differences are test placement, field names in the test fixture, and assertion depth; the migration autodetector will behave the same on all inputs.

Both patches fix django-12209 for the core case: an explicit PK given during Model.__init__ (or deserialization via Model(**data)) no longer skips the UPDATE attempt when the PK field has a default. Patch A preserves the Django 3.0 single-query INSERT optimization when the PK came from the field default by gating force_insert on pk_is_set; patch B removes that optimization entirely so every save with a set PK tries UPDATE first. They diverge on saves without an explicit constructor PK (1 vs 2 queries) and when the PK is assigned after instantiation, where only B attempts an UPDATE.

Both patches change the same five modules with the same intent: fix makemigrations model iteration (identical hunk) and replace deprecated collections ABC references with collections.abc types. On Python 3.3+, isinstance checks and Page's base class resolve to the same ABCs, so observable Django behavior matches for typical use. Patch B adds ImportError fallbacks to collections.Iterator/Iterable/Mapping on older Python; patch A hard-imports collections.abc in three modules and breaks startup on Python 2.7, while paginator differs only cosmetically.

Both patches add identical _print_Min/_print_Max methods to PythonCodePrinter that format Min/Max as Python min()/max() with recursively printed arguments. The only differences are where the methods sit in the class file (no semantic effect) and how tests are organized; A’s extra nested-expression assertions exercise the same code path both implementations use.

Both patches add identical _print_set implementations and equivalent _print_dict logic that produces the same strings for empty and non-empty dicts. The only code differences are method ordering and B's redundant empty-dict early return; neither changes dispatch or output. Test additions differ in coverage only, not in program behavior.

Both patches add the same _print_dict and _print_set handlers to ReprPrinter; _print_set is byte-identical. The sole difference in _print_dict is generator vs list comprehension before join, which yields the same string for every dict. No other files differ between A and B.

Both fix the same --pdb + @unittest.skip case by preventing pytest’s deferred tearDown from running when unittest never ran setUp/tearDown. Patch A does this by nulling _explicit_tearDown in addSkip(), while patch B guards the call in teardown() using __unittest_skip__; those differ when SkipTest is raised during a test after setUp. Patch B also changes assertion rewriting on Python 3.10+, which patch A does not.

Patches apply identical code: transform_max_iter is threaded through the mixin and three estimators into sparse_encode, and _sparse_encode only uses max_iter for lasso_cd. The sole delta is docstring wording about lasso_lars, which does not affect execution; B's docs are inaccurate but behavior matches A on every input.

Both patches fix the same bug by propagating is_summary onto the Coalesce wrapper when an aggregate has a default. Func.resolve_expression always sets c.is_summary = summarize before the Coalesce is built, and nothing in between alters c.is_summary, so summarize and c.is_summary are always equal at that point. The two assignments therefore produce identical behavior on all inputs.

Both patches identically add transform_max_iter to SparseCodingMixin and wire it through transform() into sparse_encode, and both expose it on SparseCoder with default 1000. Patch B additionally adds the constructor parameter and docs to DictionaryLearning and MiniBatchDictionaryLearning, so the two diverge only when a user sets a non-default transform_max_iter on those estimators; for defaults and SparseCoder usage, observable behavior is the same.

Both patches apply the same sliding-century logic in parse_http_date for two-digit years, including the +50 year rollback. The sole textual difference is redundant parentheses around (current_year % 100), which does not change evaluation or observable behavior on any input.

Patches A and B are byte-identical: both change the trailing free-gamma prepend loop from ascending to descending indices in kahane_simplify. A direct tree diff excluding .git shows no differences. They will produce the same observable behavior on every input.

Both patches fix the same bug in get_chunksizes by avoiding Variable.data when detecting chunking: A uses hasattr(v._data, "chunks") and B uses v.chunks is not None, which reads _data via getattr. For lazy stores and dask arrays—the inputs this issue targets—they behave the same and both pass test_chunks_does_not_load_data. They could differ only in the edge case where _data defines chunks=None, where A might still call chunksizes (which touches .data) while B skips the variable.

Both patches fix the same bug in the n_jobs==1 path by drawing n_init distinct seeds and passing each to kmeans_single instead of reusing the parent random_state. The sole difference is hoisting seed generation before the branch (A) versus duplicating it in each branch (B); for any execution only one branch runs and random_state is in the same state at the randint call, so seeds, runs, and returned labels/centers/inertia are identical.

Patches A and B are byte-identical: a single-line change adding using=db to content_type.save() in RenameContentType._rename. Recursive diff and identical git trees confirm no behavioral divergence. Both fix multi-database migrations by ensuring the ContentType rename is saved on schema_editor.connection.alias rather than the default DB.

Both patches apply the same one-line change in TableDataDiff, adding Q-format VLAs to the P-format variable-length array comparison branch. Git diffs against the common base are identical, and the patched files are byte-for-byte the same, so behavior is the same on every input.

Both patches identically switch escape() to stdlib html.escape(), so all normal escaping output matches. The only code difference is urlize()'s local unescape: A still decodes ' while B decodes ', so they diverge only when urlize processes URLs that already contain HTML-escaped apostrophes in one entity form but not the other. B aligns unescape with html.escape's output; A keeps legacy ' handling in urlize but not '.

Both patches guard the same contribute_to_class setattr so user-defined get_FOO_display on the defining class is preserved, fixing the core django-11999 bug. They differ in the guard: hasattr (A) vs cls.__dict__ (B). For a method declared on the same class they agree; they diverge when get_FOO_display comes only from a parent or mixin—A keeps the inherited method, B may replace it with the auto-generated partialmethod on the subclass.

Patches A and B are byte-identical: both replace the same two lines in trim_punctuation within django/utils/html.py with the same punctuation_length-based slicing. A recursive diff of A/ vs B/ finds no source differences. They fix urlize trailing-punctuation trimming for HTML-entity cases in the same way on every input.

Both patches are byte-for-byte identical: each adds self.save_m2m() after user.save() when commit=True in UserCreationForm.save(). That restores the M2M save step normally performed by ModelForm.save(commit=True) after the overridden save path used commit=False. No other files differ between A and B.

Patch A fixes pydata/xarray-4356 by editing `_maybe_null_out` in `nanops.py` to allow multi-axis `min_count` and compute null masks over tuple axes. Patch B never changes `nanops.py`; it only renames `np.unicode_` to `np.str_` in dtype promotion code and tests, leaving multi-dimensional `min_count` reductions raising the original ValueError. They modify disjoint logical regions with no overlapping behavioral fix for the reported bug.

Both patches fix the same MTI parent-link ordering bug by ensuring the field with parent_link=True wins when multiple OneToOneFields point at the same parent, regardless of declaration order. Patch A only collects parent_link=True fields; patch B scans all OneToOneFields but overwrites with any later parent_link=True field. They agree on the reported scenario and normal inheritance, but diverge if a model has multiple OneToOneFields to a parent with none marked parent_link=True (A auto-creates the ptr, B uses the first O2O).

Both fix the case where month-level ticks omit the year by surfacing it in the offset (e.g. the 26-week scenario). Patch A only adds that offset when no label already contains the year, while patch B always shows the offset at month level by changing show_offset suppression from level<2 to level==0. On common inputs where January (or another tick) already displays the year, A keeps the offset empty and B shows a redundant year offset, so observable behavior differs.

Both address the IndexError from calling _split_gcd with no arguments during split_surds, but A only catches it in _sqrt_match and treats it as no match (return []), while B fixes _split_gcd so split_surds completes and returns a real (g, a, b) tuple everywhere. That changes _sqrt_match/_denester control flow on those inputs and B also prevents the crash in other split_surds callers that A leaves unfixed.

Both expose transform_max_iter and forward it to sparse_encode for lasso_cd when set explicitly, but A only passes max_iter/tol when non-None while B always passes max_iter=None by default, breaking default lasso_cd behavior. A additionally wires transform_tol through _sparse_encode; B adds transform_init instead, so the patches diverge on common default usage and on tolerance or warm-start initialization inputs.

Both patches merge sqf factors with equal multiplicity in _symbolic_factor_list, but A expands first and merges unconditionally while B only merges univariate Poly factors with a common generator. The evaluation test patch requires sqf_list(x*(x + y)) to return a single combined factor; A satisfies this but B leaves separate factors, which matches B's test_factor_terms failure. They likely agree on the primary univariate bug (x-2)**3*(x-3)**3, yet diverge on multivariate and other inputs the tests cover.

Both fix the same sqf_list bug—factored vs expanded inconsistency when several factors share the same outer exponent—by ensuring square-free decomposition runs on the full polynomial content before splitting. A groups and multiplies polynomial bases by outer exponent; B expands polynomial Expr inputs first, then uses the original loop. They should agree on the canonical bug example and typical cases, but differ in mechanism and edge cases where B's is_polynomial guard skips expand while A still groups, and A's test change for x*(x+y) suggests a different expected output that its own code likely does not produce.

Both fix the same bug—Q on the left of & or | with conditional expressions like Exists—by coercing the non-Q operand into a Q before combining. For non-empty Q with Exists (the scenarios B tests and the typical report), filter semantics should align despite A double-wrapping self and B only wrapping in _combine. They diverge on empty Q() combined with a conditional non-Q, where B preserves _combine’s empty-Q identity but A’s Q(Q()) wrapper bypasses it, and on obscure reverse combinations where only A defines __rand__/__ror__.

Both patches fix the same CDS bug by making chained division left-associative, so inputs like 10+3J/m/s/kpc2 and the existing test_cds_grammar slash cases parse identically. Patch A only flips the division production while keeping right-recursive products; patch B rewrites combined_units as a single left-recursive rule for both . and /. They can diverge on rare mixed . and / strings such as m/s.km, but not on the slash-only chains this issue targets. Incidental numpy-compat edits elsewhere are largely aligned and not the main semantic difference.

Both adjust when ConciseDateFormatter shows the year offset for sub-year axes, but B only stops hiding it for month-level ticks (level < 1), while A removes that guard and suppresses the offset whenever a tick label already includes a year. For common January-start ranges, including the ~26-week case B's test expects to show '1997', A typically still yields an empty offset, and for ~1-year month axes B can show a duplicate year offset that A omits.

Both patches allow in_bulk on single-field Meta.UniqueConstraint instead of only field.unique, and behave identically for unconditional constraints. Patch A uses total_unique_constraints (condition is None), while Patch B accepts any UniqueConstraint on that field, so partial/conditional constraints pass validation in B but raise ValueError in A, and B can return wrong one-to-one mappings when the field is not globally unique.

Both fix Q.deconstruct() for a sole non-tuple expression child (e.g. Exists) by putting it in args instead of mis-treating it as kwargs. B narrows the old kwargs optimization to length-2 tuples; A drops it entirely, changing serialized form for ordinary single-kwarg Q while B keeps it. Reconstructed Q objects agree for typical filters and Q(expression), but deconstruct() output differs for common Q(lookup=value) and B still drops _connector when the kwargs shortcut applies.

Both add the same Q._combine wrapping for conditional expressions, so non-empty Q & Exists and Exists & Q behave alike. Patch A also fixes Q.deconstruct for expression children, which is required when empty Q() combines and calls deconstruct on Q(Exists); patch B only adds Combinable.__rand__/__ror__, which does not run for Q().__and__(Exists). They diverge on Q() & Exists and on deconstruct/pickle of Q objects holding expressions.

Both patches fix the same pytest-5787/5792 bug by serializing and deserializing ExceptionChainRepr.chain in reports.py, so typical chained test failures should round-trip with identical longrepr output. B refactors shared helpers, uses dict-based chain encoding instead of A's tuple triples, and adds null-safe reprcrash handling plus a regression test. They diverge on rare None-reprcrash cases and produce incompatible serialized formats, but are not meaningfully different for normal chained exceptions with tracebacks.

Both patches fix issue 7067 in _symbolic_factor_list with the same test, but A post-combines all factors sharing a multiplicity after per-Mul-argument processing, whereas B short-circuits to whole-polynomial poly.sqf_list() when polification succeeds with symbol generators. They agree on motivating cases like x*(x+y) and (x-2)**3*(x-3)**3, but A incorrectly merges coprime square-free factors at the same multiplicity (e.g. (x-1)*(x+1)), and they differ when polification fails or gens are non-symbols, where only A's post-combine runs.

Both patches adjust the same `_eval_subs` simplification when the condition becomes `S.true`, but A always returns the base set while B keeps the old `ConditionSet(new, Contains(new, base), base)` path whenever `new` is a Symbol. They match for non-Symbol substitutions, yet disagree on documented cases such as `ConditionSet(n, n < x, Interval(-oo, 0)).subs(x, p)`, producing `Interval(-oo, 0)` versus `S.EmptySet`.

Both patches add _merge_factors and apply it to sqf_list results in _generic_factor_list to combine duplicate multiplicity levels arising from Mul-based factorization. Patch A merges all factors with the same k unconditionally (dict-based, before sorting); patch B merges only adjacent, same-k pairs where both factors are univariate (after sorting). They should behave the same for purely univariate inputs with repeated multiplicities, but diverge on multivariate or mixed cases—e.g. sqf_list(x*(x + y)) yields one merged factor in A versus two separate factors in B—and B is a stricter refinement rather than a strict superset of A.

Patch A removes the `.[`→`[` substitution so parametrized ids containing `.[` are preserved in `getmodpath()`/`reportinfo()`, which is the intended fix for pytest-6202. Patch B keeps (and triple-applies) the same substitution, so it still mangles those ids and can worsen cases like the `"..["` repro. The two agree only when the joined modpath has no `.[` substring; B’s extra `bug.py` does not change library semantics.

Both patches fix deparented-artist cleanup in the same clear() paths, and their Figure.clear() loops are semantically aligned. They diverge on the common ax.plot()+cla()/clf() path: A unsets both .axes and .figure on _children and clears child_axes._axes, while B only unsets .axes. That leaves stale .figure references after cla/clf in B but not A, which is central to the bug.

Both patches add violation_error_code to Django constraints with nearly identical BaseConstraint/CheckConstraint/UniqueConstraint(expression) changes. They diverge on feature-relevant inputs: A wires violation_error_code through postgres ExclusionConstraint while B does not; B overrides the ValidationError code for field-based UniqueConstraint when violation_error_code is set (and adds positional-arg deprecation support), while A leaves field-based violations using unique_error_message()'s default code unchanged.

Both patches implement the same violation_error_code feature end-to-end: init, deconstruct, equality, and ValidationError raising with the custom code. The sole divergence is repr output when violation_error_code is non-None—A includes it, B does not—so observable strings can differ while runtime validation behavior stays the same.

Both patches apply the same fix to k_means in sklearn/cluster/k_means_.py: precompute n_init integer seeds before the n_jobs branch and pass each seed to kmeans_single in the single-thread loop instead of reusing the advancing random_state object. Patch A adds two comments; B has identical runtime logic. No input can produce different observable behavior between A and B.

Both patches split datetime and timedelta handling in _ensure_numeric, fixing the bug where a datetime64 offset was applied to timedelta data. The sole semantic difference is timedelta offset: patch A uses zero (absolute ns values), while patch B uses datetime_to_numeric's default minimum offset. That shifts all coordinates when min(timedelta) != 0, so polyval and other numeric uses diverge from polyfit's unshifted astype(float) x values on typical timedelta coordinates.

Both add the same _split_type_and_name helper and use it in DocFieldTransformer for typed :param type name: fields, fixing parenthesis-nested types like dict(str, str) name identically. The sole semantic difference is that A also tracks square-bracket depth, so bracket annotations with internal whitespace (e.g. dict[str, str] x, Callable[[int, str], None] cb) parse correctly in A but mis-split in B, changing rendered param types for those inputs.

Both patches apply the same RFC 7231 sliding 50-year window to two-digit HTTP years, replacing the old fixed 70 pivot. Production logic differs only in using utcnow().year versus now().year for the reference year, which can diverge when the local calendar year differs from UTC (mainly around New Year in non-UTC timezones). On typical UTC servers and for most inputs they behave the same; tests differ in mocking style but cover the same rule.

Diffs against the common base and a recursive diff of A/ vs B/ (excluding .git) are identical: both move seed generation before the n_jobs branch and pass per-run integer seeds in the single-threaded loop. There is no semantic divergence between the two candidates.

Both patches target the same KeyboardTransform bug and produce identical output for typical multi-key sequences including the core regression case Shift-+. They diverge on separator-only :kbd: values (-, +, ^), where A leaves the node untransformed and B applies a nested kbd transform, and on some edge-case strings where a separator precedes or abuts alphanumerics without a prior key name.

Both patches add violation_error_code and route constraint failures through get_violation_error(), and their CheckConstraint/UniqueConstraint validation paths match when init succeeds. Patch A defines default_violation_error_code = None on BaseConstraint; patch B does not but still assigns self.default_violation_error_code in __init__, so creating a constraint without an explicit code raises AttributeError in B but works in A. Remaining deconstruct/repr guards differ only if a non-None class default existed, which neither patch uses.

Both patches fix the same KeyboardTransform bug—empty key slots from regex split when -, +, or ^ are keystrokes—and produce identical decompositions for standard compounds like Shift-+ and Control+X. They diverge on standalone separators (A skips the transform after merge, leaving a flat kbd node; B always rebuilds with a nested inner kbd) and on edge inputs such as -Ctrl or A-+B where A merges tokens and B does not.

Both patches apply the identical two-line fix in Dataset.swap_dims: shallow-copy variables with copy(deep=False) before assigning new dims, so the original Dataset is not mutated in place. DataArray.swap_dims inherits this via delegation. The only differences are ancillary—B adds an in-tree regression test while A adds root-level debug scripts—neither affects runtime semantics.

Both catch IndexError from split_surds when no Pow surd factors exist, but A falls through to depth-based _sqrt_match while B returns None and skips denesting. When split_surds succeeds they are identical; on the fail-to-pass expression sqrtdenest((3 - sqrt(2)*sqrt(4 + 3*I) + 3*I)/2) only A reaches I while B leaves it unchanged.

Both patches fix the same bug by shallow-copying when to_index_variable() returns the original IndexVariable before mutating var.dims in the k-in-result_dims branch. Patch A also guards the else branch after to_base_variable(), but that method always returns a new Variable, so the extra guard never runs and behavior matches B on all real inputs.

Both patches fix the same polyval bug by using 1-D coordinate values instead of datetime data and by handling timedelta64 separately from datetime64 in _ensure_numeric. On the issue’s zero-based timedelta coordinate they agree, but A uses a fixed zero offset while B subtracts the array minimum (diverging when min ≠ 0), and only A reorders Horner/transpose output dimensions. A also includes an unrelated dtypes.py change.

Both patches add the same `_print_Indexed` method that formats indexed access as `base[i, j, ...]`. Patch A additionally adds `_print_IndexedBase` so bases print via the Python code printer; patch B relies on the `_print_not_supported` fallback, which usually yields the same subscript string for simple symbol names but adds spurious "Not supported" header comments and may differ for reserved identifiers or complex labels.

Both aim to fix polyval with datetime/timedelta coords but use different numeric pipelines. Patch A reuses get_clean_interp_index (matching polyfit) for named DataArray coordinates; patch B switches _ensure_numeric to min-relative datetime_to_numeric. They agree on ordinary numeric inputs but diverge for timedelta coords, for datetime coords not anchored at the series minimum, for Dataset coords, and when coordinate values differ from data values.

Patch A rewrites _eval_is_zero to accumulate and sum real and imaginary coefficients, which can detect cancelling imaginary terms. Patch B only adds a comment and keeps the original im/im_or_z flag logic that still returns False when imaginary terms cancel but the real partial sum is zero. B never applied its draft summation algorithm to add.py, so the two patches behave differently on the bug and most related inputs.

Both patches fix the same deconstruct bug by only treating a lone child as kwargs when it is a length-2 tuple, and otherwise putting it in args. For normal Q usage (kwarg lookups, multiple children, nested Q, and expression children with the default AND connector) the returned deconstruct triple is identical. The only behavioral gap is an edge case with a single non-tuple child and a non-default connector, where B also emits _connector and A does not.

Both patches auto-set levels=[0.5] for boolean Z in the common contour(Z) and contour(X,Y,Z) cases when levels are omitted. They diverge on contourf (A uses [0,1], B leaves default autolevels), on contour(bool_Z, positional_levels) where B overwrites user levels but A does not, and on non-ndarray bool inputs where only B's np.asarray check applies.

Both patches add __repr__ to _RepeatedSplits for the same repr bug and leave split/get_n_splits logic identical. Patch B reuses _build_repr, which only reads attributes on self and omits constructor kwargs stored in self.cvargs, so repr shows n_splits=None while patch A correctly pulls n_splits from cvargs. They also differ in parameter ordering/formatting, so repr() diverges on the main RepeatedKFold/RepeatedStratifiedKFold inputs even though CV behavior matches.

Both patches implement the same violation_error_code feature on core model constraints with equivalent validate/deconstruct/eq/repr behavior, differing only in style (get_violation_error helper vs inline ValidationError). Patch A additionally wires violation_error_code through postgres ExclusionConstraint, while B omits that file entirely, so custom codes on exclusion constraints work only in A. For field-based UniqueConstraint without a custom code, B always re-wraps unique_error_message errors whereas A preserves the original object, but observable message/code/params should match.

Both patches implement the same fix: group fast-delete querysets by model, combine with OR, and issue one _raw_delete per model. The combine helpers are semantically the same (B's single-queryset branch and empty-list guard are optimizations only), and the test updates assert identical query counts. Method placement and comment wording differ but do not change deletion behavior.

Both fix left-associative chained division for the reported strings like J/m/s/kpc2, but A keeps product grouping on the RHS of a slash while B applies a single left-to-right rule for both . and /, so mixed inputs such as J/m.s differ. Patch B additionally changes Quantity, NumPy helpers, SI curie, and shapes behavior unrelated to the CDS bug, so the two trees are not behaviorally equivalent overall.

Both patches change the same line from `level < 2` to a condition that is true only when level is 0 (year-level ticks). For integer levels from the loop, `level < 1` and `level == 0` are logically identical, so show_offset is suppressed only for year-varying ticks in both cases. No other code differs between A and B.

Both patches implement the same RenameIndex.database_backwards path for old_fields: locate the index by columns, compute the auto-generated name, and rename it back, with identical error handling and allow_migrate checks. A vs B differs only in comments and trivial formatting; the test changes exercise the same forward/backward/forward cycle with the same assertions.

Both fix the core datetime-data + timedelta-coordinate case when the DataArray name matches the coordinate, but A selects coordinates by dimension (including numeric coords) before conversion while B only handles a named datetime-like coordinate after _ensure_numeric. They also differ on timedelta-only conversion (offset=None vs epoch) and on named datetime arrays with same-kind datetime coordinates, so polyval can disagree on several realistic inputs beyond the primary regression.

Both patches add the same pipe token to _token_regex and fix typing.py for Python 3.10+ UnionType, which fully fixes PEP 604 unions for NumPy docstrings via the existing _convert_numpy_type_spec path. Patch B additionally rewrites _convert_type_spec so Google-style napoleon fields with | get per-type :class:/:obj: markup; patch A would still wrap the whole string as one class name. For the tested NumPy union cases the observable output matches; they diverge only on Google-format docstrings using | with type preprocessing enabled.

Both patches prevent swap_dims from mutating in-place variables still referenced by the caller when an IndexVariable is converted via to_index_variable(). Patch A copies only when conversion returns the same object; Patch B always shallow-copies first, which is redundant when conversion already returns a new wrapper but does not change observable swap_dims results. The tests differ only in comments and assertion style, not in the behavior under test.

Both patches replace the same split in DocFieldTransformer with a helper aimed at parenthesized types containing spaces, and they agree on the main bug case (e.g. dict(str, str) name) and simple type name pairs. They diverge when types have nested or multiple parenthesis groups—A's regex splits at the first ') ' while B uses parenthesis depth—and B strips whitespace from the name whereas A may not. Those edge cases are uncommon in real docstrings, so behavioral overlap is high but not total.

Both patches add the same empty-surds guard to avoid crashing in _split_gcd, but they return different (a, b) values: A returns (1, 0, expr) matching the normal path where all terms land in b, while B swaps to (1, expr, 0). Callers like rad_rationalize and _sqrtdenest_rec unpack (g, a, b) and perform different algebra whenever this early return runs on a nonzero input.

Both patches apply the identical two-line guard in Dataset.swap_dims before assigning var.dims. They touch only xarray/core/dataset.py and differ in no source files. The fix handles IndexVariable.to_index_variable() returning self by shallow-copying so dimension swaps do not mutate the original dataset coordinate.

Both patches are byte-identical: they add the same __repr__ on _RepeatedSplits that calls _build_repr(self), matching other cross-validators in _split.py. The full A and B trees differ only in git metadata, not source. The change affects string representation only, not split or get_n_splits behavior.

Both patches add the same ForeignKey.db_parameters collation propagation, so CREATE and explicit FK alters behave the same. Patch A additionally teaches the schema editor’s rels_to_update path to apply collation when related columns are updated during PK/type changes; patch B omits that, so altering a collated PK (e.g. AutoField to CharField with db_collation) can leave referencing FK columns without matching collation unless the FK is altered separately.

Both patches apply the same real/imaginary decomposition fix to Add._eval_is_zero and should agree on the complex-cancellation bug (e.g. -2*I + (1+I)**2). They diverge mainly when all arguments are known non-zero reals: B preserves the base early return of None, while A can return False after summing those reals. Imaginary coefficient extraction also differs slightly (division with is_real checks vs -I*a), which may matter on edge-case assumptions.

Patches A and B are byte-identical: each adds the same two lines in Dataset.swap_dims after to_index_variable(), copying with deep=False only when var is v before assigning var.dims. Recursive diff of A/ and B/ shows no file differences outside .git. Both fix in-place mutation of IndexVariable coordinates during swap_dims with the same semantics on every input.

Both patches add the same Q._combine logic to wrap non-Q conditional expressions (e.g. Exists) in Q() before combining, fixing Q-on-the-left and Exists combinations. The sole code difference is truthy vs `is True` on conditional, which is equivalent for all built-in Django expressions where conditional is always bool True/False. Test changes only reorder identical assertions.

Both patches implement the same sliding 50-year window for two-digit HTTP years instead of the old fixed year<70 rule. Patch A anchors on the current century (year - year%100); patch B hardcodes +2000 before the same rollback check. For system years 2000–2099 they are identical on all two-digit inputs; they only diverge when the clock is outside that range, which is not realistic for normal Django use.

Both replace the old_fields backward no-op with a reverse rename and similar tests, but they disagree on the restored auto-generated index name: A uses suffix "_idx" (correct for index_together) while B uses "_uniq" (unique-constraint naming). On rollback of unnamed index_together renames, the database ends up with different index names, so behavior diverges on the main inputs even though the overall fix intent overlaps.

Patches A and B produce identical source trees: the same unified diff against the base, matching git tree hashes, and byte-identical django/db/models/constraints.py. Both add violation_error_code plumbing and pass it to ValidationError on constraint violations in the same places. Only commit metadata differs; runtime behavior is the same on every input.

Both patches only add __repr__ to _RepeatedSplits, leaving split/get_n_splits unchanged, so cross-validation behavior is identical. Repr output matches for the usual int/None parameters; it can differ when random_state is a RandomState instance because patch A uses repr (!r) and patch B uses str.

disagreements.agent_reasoning: Both patches fix the same bug by passing multi_class into the temporary LogisticRegression inside _log_reg_scoring_path, so multinomial LogisticRegressionCV scoring uses softmax probabilities instead of OvR. The only library code difference is that A also forwards random_state to that helper instance, which is never used because fit is not called before scoring. Extra ad-hoc test scripts in A do not change sklearn runtime behavior.

Both patches add the same guard in ConditionSet._eval_subs when substitution yields cond is S.true. Patch A routes through ConditionSet(sym, S.true, base), which __new__ immediately reduces to base_set; patch B returns base directly. That is the only library change and matches the issue-19495 fix, so observable substitution behavior is the same on all inputs where the condition is exactly S.true.

Both skip Value() wrapping for F(), combined F expressions, and Func-based expressions, fixing the same bulk_update bug. The sole logic difference is expression detection: isinstance(Expression, F) versus hasattr(resolve_expression), which only diverges for rare BaseExpression types like Subquery. Test changes are cosmetic variants of the same F() scenarios.

Both patches replace the hardcoded 70/1900–2000 century split in parse_http_date with the same RFC 7231 sliding 50-year window logic, differing only in whether the anchor year is taken from utcnow() (A) or now() (B). That one-line choice is behaviorally equivalent in many common cases but diverges for two-digit years when local and UTC years differ or when tests pin utcnow to 2019/2020/2048 while B still reads the real local year. A matches the gold fix and intended GMT-relative behavior; B is the same fix idea with a wrong clock reference.

Both patches fix the same bug in Add._eval_is_zero by replacing the boolean im flag with a list of imaginary terms and, when real parts cancel, checking whether the imaginary parts cancel instead of always returning False. The sole semantic difference is whether cancellation is tested on a/I (A) or on the raw imaginary terms (B); for is_imaginary args these are equivalent in principle but may differ slightly on unevaluated symbolic sums due to different simplification paths.

The production fix in astropy/io/fits/diff.py is byte-identical in both trees: Q-format VLA columns join the same per-element comparison path as P-format columns. Patch B only adds a regression test in test_diff.py, which does not change runtime FITS-diff behavior. For all user-facing inputs, including Q-format VLA tables, the two patches produce the same observable results.

Both patches change the same offset-hiding logic in ConciseDateFormatter.format_ticks: hide the offset at year level (level 0), and at month level (level 1) only when a January tick (month==1) will show the year in a label. Patch A uses nested if level < 2 / level == 0 / else; patch B uses if level == 0 / elif level == 1 with the identical January check. No other files differ; observable tick/offset formatting is the same for all inputs.

Both candidates apply the identical one-line production fix in TableDataDiff, extending the VLA branch to include Q-format columns alongside P-format. diff.py is byte-identical between A and B. B adds a regression test only; that does not change FITSDiff behavior on any user input.

Both patches identically add transform_max_iter to SparseCodingMixin and wire it through transform() into sparse_encode, and both expose it on SparseCoder with default 1000. Patch B additionally adds the constructor parameter and docs to DictionaryLearning and MiniBatchDictionaryLearning, so the two diverge only when a user sets a non-default transform_max_iter on those estimators; for defaults and SparseCoder usage, observable behavior is the same.

Both patches fix the same bug in get_chunksizes by avoiding Variable.data when detecting chunking: A uses hasattr(v._data, "chunks") and B uses v.chunks is not None, which reads _data via getattr. For lazy stores and dask arrays—the inputs this issue targets—they behave the same and both pass test_chunks_does_not_load_data. They could differ only in the edge case where _data defines chunks=None, where A might still call chunksizes (which touches .data) while B skips the variable.

Both patches identically switch escape() to stdlib html.escape(), so all normal escaping output matches. The only code difference is urlize()'s local unescape: A still decodes ' while B decodes ', so they diverge only when urlize processes URLs that already contain HTML-escaped apostrophes in one entity form but not the other. B aligns unescape with html.escape's output; A keeps legacy ' handling in urlize but not '.

Both patches guard the same contribute_to_class setattr so user-defined get_FOO_display on the defining class is preserved, fixing the core django-11999 bug. They differ in the guard: hasattr (A) vs cls.__dict__ (B). For a method declared on the same class they agree; they diverge when get_FOO_display comes only from a parent or mixin—A keeps the inherited method, B may replace it with the auto-generated partialmethod on the subclass.

Both patches add violation_error_code to Django constraints with nearly identical BaseConstraint/CheckConstraint/UniqueConstraint(expression) changes. They diverge on feature-relevant inputs: A wires violation_error_code through postgres ExclusionConstraint while B does not; B overrides the ValidationError code for field-based UniqueConstraint when violation_error_code is set (and adds positional-arg deprecation support), while A leaves field-based violations using unique_error_message()'s default code unchanged.

Both patches implement the same violation_error_code feature end-to-end: init, deconstruct, equality, and ValidationError raising with the custom code. The sole divergence is repr output when violation_error_code is non-None—A includes it, B does not—so observable strings can differ while runtime validation behavior stays the same.

Both patches split datetime and timedelta handling in _ensure_numeric, fixing the bug where a datetime64 offset was applied to timedelta data. The sole semantic difference is timedelta offset: patch A uses zero (absolute ns values), while patch B uses datetime_to_numeric's default minimum offset. That shifts all coordinates when min(timedelta) != 0, so polyval and other numeric uses diverge from polyfit's unshifted astype(float) x values on typical timedelta coordinates.

Both add the same _split_type_and_name helper and use it in DocFieldTransformer for typed :param type name: fields, fixing parenthesis-nested types like dict(str, str) name identically. The sole semantic difference is that A also tracks square-bracket depth, so bracket annotations with internal whitespace (e.g. dict[str, str] x, Callable[[int, str], None] cb) parse correctly in A but mis-split in B, changing rendered param types for those inputs.

Both patches add violation_error_code and route constraint failures through get_violation_error(), and their CheckConstraint/UniqueConstraint validation paths match when init succeeds. Patch A defines default_violation_error_code = None on BaseConstraint; patch B does not but still assigns self.default_violation_error_code in __init__, so creating a constraint without an explicit code raises AttributeError in B but works in A. Remaining deconstruct/repr guards differ only if a non-None class default existed, which neither patch uses.

Both patches fix the same KeyboardTransform bug—empty key slots from regex split when -, +, or ^ are keystrokes—and produce identical decompositions for standard compounds like Shift-+ and Control+X. They diverge on standalone separators (A skips the transform after merge, leaving a flat kbd node; B always rebuilds with a nested inner kbd) and on edge inputs such as -Ctrl or A-+B where A merges tokens and B does not.

Both patches apply the identical two-line fix in Dataset.swap_dims: shallow-copy variables with copy(deep=False) before assigning new dims, so the original Dataset is not mutated in place. DataArray.swap_dims inherits this via delegation. The only differences are ancillary—B adds an in-tree regression test while A adds root-level debug scripts—neither affects runtime semantics.

Both catch IndexError from split_surds when no Pow surd factors exist, but A falls through to depth-based _sqrt_match while B returns None and skips denesting. When split_surds succeeds they are identical; on the fail-to-pass expression sqrtdenest((3 - sqrt(2)*sqrt(4 + 3*I) + 3*I)/2) only A reaches I while B leaves it unchanged.

Both patches fix the same bug by shallow-copying when to_index_variable() returns the original IndexVariable before mutating var.dims in the k-in-result_dims branch. Patch A also guards the else branch after to_base_variable(), but that method always returns a new Variable, so the extra guard never runs and behavior matches B on all real inputs.

Both patches add the same `_print_Indexed` method that formats indexed access as `base[i, j, ...]`. Patch A additionally adds `_print_IndexedBase` so bases print via the Python code printer; patch B relies on the `_print_not_supported` fallback, which usually yields the same subscript string for simple symbol names but adds spurious "Not supported" header comments and may differ for reserved identifiers or complex labels.

Both patches fix the same deconstruct bug by only treating a lone child as kwargs when it is a length-2 tuple, and otherwise putting it in args. For normal Q usage (kwarg lookups, multiple children, nested Q, and expression children with the default AND connector) the returned deconstruct triple is identical. The only behavioral gap is an edge case with a single non-tuple child and a non-default connector, where B also emits _connector and A does not.

Both patches implement the same fix: group fast-delete querysets by model, combine with OR, and issue one _raw_delete per model. The combine helpers are semantically the same (B's single-queryset branch and empty-list guard are optimizations only), and the test updates assert identical query counts. Method placement and comment wording differ but do not change deletion behavior.

Both patches add the same empty-surds guard to avoid crashing in _split_gcd, but they return different (a, b) values: A returns (1, 0, expr) matching the normal path where all terms land in b, while B swaps to (1, expr, 0). Callers like rad_rationalize and _sqrtdenest_rec unpack (g, a, b) and perform different algebra whenever this early return runs on a nonzero input.

Both patches apply the same real/imaginary decomposition fix to Add._eval_is_zero and should agree on the complex-cancellation bug (e.g. -2*I + (1+I)**2). They diverge mainly when all arguments are known non-zero reals: B preserves the base early return of None, while A can return False after summing those reals. Imaginary coefficient extraction also differs slightly (division with is_real checks vs -I*a), which may matter on edge-case assumptions.

Both patches implement the same sliding 50-year window for two-digit HTTP years instead of the old fixed year<70 rule. Patch A anchors on the current century (year - year%100); patch B hardcodes +2000 before the same rollback check. For system years 2000–2099 they are identical on all two-digit inputs; they only diverge when the clock is outside that range, which is not realistic for normal Django use.

Both replace the old_fields backward no-op with a reverse rename and similar tests, but they disagree on the restored auto-generated index name: A uses suffix "_idx" (correct for index_together) while B uses "_uniq" (unique-constraint naming). On rollback of unnamed index_together renames, the database ends up with different index names, so behavior diverges on the main inputs even though the overall fix intent overlaps.
